metadata.subject: Mathematics
metadata.description: Apprenticeship Mathematics 12 prepares students for trades, technical, and skilled-trades careers by developing practical mathematical skills applied in real workplace contexts. Students build proficiency in measurement, geometry, trigonometry, finance, and data analysis through problems drawn from construction, automotive, electrical, culinary, healthcare, and other trades and technical fields. The course emphasizes mathematical reasoning, estimation, and problem solving in authentic professional contexts. First Peoples mathematical knowledge and land-based measurement traditions are integrated throughout.
metadata.general_description: A workplace-focused mathematics course developing practical skills in measurement, geometry, trigonometry, and finance through authentic trades and technical applications — preparing students for apprenticeship and skilled-trades careers.

big_ideas.statement: Mathematics in trades and technical fields requires precision, estimation, and contextual reasoning.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students develop the mathematical habits of mind that effective tradespeople use — including knowing when to estimate, when to calculate precisely, and how to check the reasonableness of results.

big_ideas.statement: Measurement and geometry are foundational to skilled trades and technical work.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students apply measurement systems, geometric reasoning, and spatial visualization to solve problems encountered in construction, manufacturing, and technical fields.

big_ideas.statement: Financial literacy is essential for managing personal and business finances in skilled trades careers.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students develop practical financial skills — including wage calculation, budgeting, taxation, and business costs — relevant to self-employment and trades apprenticeship.

big_ideas.statement: Mathematics is embedded in Indigenous practices of construction, navigation, measurement, and land stewardship.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students recognize Indigenous mathematical knowledge — including traditional measurement, architecture, and land management — as sophisticated systems developed through centuries of practical experience.

competencies.raw: Use logic and patterns to solve puzzles and play games; use reasoning and logic to explore, analyze, and apply mathematical ideas; estimate reasonably; demonstrate and apply mental math strategies; use tools or technology to explore and create patterns and relationships; model mathematics in contextualized experiences.
i_can_statements: I can apply logical reasoning to analyze and solve trades and technical mathematics problems.
I can estimate quantities, measurements, and costs in workplace contexts and evaluate the reasonableness of results.
I can model mathematical relationships using diagrams, tables, formulas, and technology.
I can recognize and apply mathematical patterns in trades and technical applications.

competencies.raw: Apply multiple strategies to solve problems in both abstract and contextualized situations; develop, demonstrate, and apply mathematical understanding through play, inquiry, and problem solving; visualize to explore mathematical concepts; engage in problem-solving experiences that are connected to place, story, cultural practices, and perspectives.
i_can_statements: I can apply multiple strategies to solve measurement, geometry, and financial problems in trades contexts.
I can visualize and sketch geometric problems before solving them.
I can connect mathematical problems to real workplace scenarios, including Indigenous land-based contexts.
I can develop and test solution strategies for unfamiliar applied problems.

competencies.raw: Use mathematical vocabulary and language to contribute to mathematical discussions; explain and justify mathematical ideas and decisions; communicate mathematical thinking in many ways; represent mathematical ideas in concrete, pictorial, and symbolic forms.
i_can_statements: I can explain my mathematical thinking and justify my solutions using precise trades vocabulary and mathematical language.
I can represent mathematical problems and solutions using sketches, formulas, tables, and technology.
I can communicate mathematical ideas clearly in professional and workplace-relevant contexts.

competencies.raw: Reflect on mathematical thinking; connect mathematical concepts to each other and to other areas and personal interests; use mathematical arguments to support personal choices; incorporate First Peoples worldviews and perspectives to make connections to mathematical concepts.
i_can_statements: I can connect mathematical concepts to trades, technical fields, and personal career interests.
I can reflect on the effectiveness of my mathematical strategies and identify improvements.
I can recognize mathematical knowledge embedded in First Peoples practices and connect it to course concepts.
I can use mathematical reasoning to support informed personal and professional decisions.

areas.name: Measurement
areas.raw: Measurement systems, precision, and applied measurement in trades contexts.
topics: Imperial and SI measurement systems: conversions, tools, and trades applications
Precision and accuracy: significant figures, tolerances, and acceptable measurement error in trades
Linear measurement: perimeter, circumference, and length calculations in construction and manufacturing
Area measurement: calculating area for flooring, roofing, painting, and land use
Volume measurement: calculating volume for concrete, soil, liquids, and storage
Three-dimensional measurement: applying measurement to containers, tanks, and structural elements

areas.name: Geometry and Spatial Reasoning
areas.raw: Applied geometry, trigonometry, and spatial reasoning for trades.
topics: 2D geometry review: angles, polygons, circles, and their properties in trades applications
Right triangle trigonometry: SOH-CAH-TOA applied to roofing, framing, and electrical conduit bending
Pythagorean theorem: applied to construction layouts, stair design, and diagonal measurement
3D geometry: surface area and volume of prisms, cylinders, cones, and composite shapes
Scale drawings and blueprints: reading, interpreting, and applying architectural and technical drawings
Spatial visualization: interpreting three-dimensional objects from two-dimensional plans

areas.name: Finance and Business Mathematics
areas.raw: Personal and business finance for trades and self-employment.
topics: Wages and income: hourly, salary, piece-rate, commission, and overtime calculations
Taxes and deductions: income tax, CPP, EI, and net versus gross pay
Budgeting: creating and managing personal and project budgets
Material and job costing: estimating material quantities, costs, markup, and profit margin
GST, PST, and invoicing: tax calculations and professional invoicing for trades businesses
Simple and compound interest: loans, mortgages, and financing tools for self-employed tradespeople

areas.name: Data Analysis and Statistics
areas.raw: Reading and interpreting data in trades and technical contexts.
topics: Reading technical data: interpreting charts, tables, specifications, and data sheets
Statistical measures: mean, median, mode, and range applied to quality control and productivity
Data visualization: constructing and interpreting bar, line, and pie charts for business data
Probability basics: understanding risk, safety statistics, and probabilistic reasoning in trades
Spreadsheet tools: using Excel or Google Sheets for job costing, data tracking, and analysis
Interpreting research data: reading health and safety statistics, material specifications, and test results

areas.name: First Peoples Mathematical Knowledge
areas.raw: Indigenous mathematics, measurement traditions, and land-based knowledge systems.
topics: Traditional measurement systems: body-based and land-based measurement practices of BC First Peoples
Architectural knowledge: traditional construction of longhouses, pit houses, and their geometric properties
Navigation and wayfinding: mathematical knowledge embedded in traditional travel and spatial orientation
Sustainable resource management: mathematical thinking in traditional fishing, harvesting, and land stewardship
Cultural patterns and geometry: geometric patterns in weaving, carving, and Indigenous art
Connecting TEK to modern trades: how traditional knowledge informs contemporary sustainable building and resource management
